content.problemStatement:
You are given a **row-wise sorted 2D matrix** of size N×MN \times MN×M. Your task is to find the **median** of the matrix.

The **median** is the middle element after sorting all elements of the matrix in **non-decreasing order**. It is guaranteed that N×MN \times MN×M is **odd**, so the median always exists.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindMedianfindMedianfindMedian – This function finds the median of a row-wise sorted matrix.

Parameters
matrixmatrixmatrix : A 2D array of integers of size N×MN \times MN×M, where each row is sorted in non-decreasing order.
NNN : The number of rows in the matrix.
MMM : The number of columns in the matrix.
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the **median** of the matrix.
Constraints
1≤N,M≤1051 ≤ N, M ≤ 10^51≤N,M≤105
1≤N×M≤1061 ≤ N \times M ≤ 10^61≤N×M≤106
1≤matrix[i][j]≤1091 \leq matrix[i][j] \leq 10^91≤matrix[i][j]≤109
N×MN \times MN×M is **odd**
Each row of the matrix is sorted in non-decreasing order

Input Format
The first line contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of rows and columns of the matrix.


Each of the next NNN lines contains MMM space-separated integers, denoting the elements of the matrix.



Output Format
Print a single integer — the **median** of the matrix.
constraints: Each row of the matrix is sorted in non‑decreasing order
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and M denoting rows and columns. Next N lines each contain M space‑separated integers representing a row of the matrix (each row is sorted in non‑decreasing order).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer – the median of the given matrix – on its own line.

solution.approach: Because each row is sorted, we can binary‑search on the value domain (the smallest element to the largest element) instead of flattening the matrix. For a candidate value x we count how many elements are ≤ x using upper_bound on every row (O(log M) per row). If the count is less than the required position ( (N*M+1)/2 ) we need a larger value, otherwise x can be the answer. The search converges to the smallest value with count ≥ required, which is exactly the median.
solution.timeComplexity: O( log(MaxValue‑MinValue) * N * log M ) per test case, where MaxValue‑MinValue ≤ 10^9. This fits easily within the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M) to store the matrix (input size). Apart from that only O(1) extra space.
